title: Good Enough
subtitle: The most dangerous words in business are "we could improve this"[I]... by involving a bear[/I]

sections.content: There is a man in your office who has reorganised the shared drive three times this year. Each time he presents the new structure to the team with the energy of someone who has solved a problem that matters. Each time the team nods politely, moves their files into the new folders, and continues doing their actual work. The shared drive was fine before. It is fine now. It will be fine after his next reorganisation, which is already being planned, because the man has confused activity with progress and no one has told him to stop.

He is optimising a module that doesn't need optimising. And the thing he should be working on — the thing that is actually broken, actually losing money, actually causing people to stay late on Thursdays — sits untouched, because fixing real problems is harder and less satisfying than rearranging folders.
This is one of the most common and least discussed failures in business, in personal productivity, and in life: improving the wrong thing.
Hegemonica

The Roman statesman Cicero used the term hegemonica to describe the governing part of the soul — the part that is in charge, that directs, that should not be tampered with lightly. We're going to borrow the word and repurpose it for something more practical.
Hegemonica: that which cannot or should not be improved upon.

Not "perfect." Not "the best it could ever be." Just: good enough, in a way that deserves respect rather than interference. When you identify something as hegemonica, you are acknowledging that it is functioning, it is not the bottleneck, and your attention belongs elsewhere.

This is harder than it sounds. The instinct to optimise is strong, especially in people who are good at their jobs. When you can see how something could be better, leaving it alone feels negligent. It feels like laziness. It feels like you're accepting mediocrity. But the ability to look at something adequate and walk away from it is not laziness. It's triage. And triage is one of the most valuable skills a person can develop.
The System Has Parts

Everything you interact with — your job, your morning routine, your business, your health, your kitchen — is a system made of modules. A system exists to serve a purpose. The modules exist to serve the system's purpose. How the modules are connected to each other — their integration — determines whether the system achieves that purpose or collapses under the weight of its own complexity.

Consider a bridge. The purpose of a bridge is to move people and goods across terrain that would otherwise be impassable. Pylons raise the bridge above the water. Roads allow vehicles to cross. Lights and signs guide the way to increase safety. Every module exists to serve the bridge's purpose.

But some modules don't directly move people across the bridge. The lights don't carry traffic — they exist so that the road can carry traffic safely at night. The barrier in the center doesn't transport anything — it exists so that opposing lanes of traffic can be integrated without colliding. These are integration modules. They don't do the primary work. They make it possible for the modules that do the primary work to function together without destroying each other.

Here's where people go wrong: if you forget the purpose of the bridge and become fixated on the performance of the lights, you might install the brightest lights available. More light equals better performance, right? Except now the lights are blinding the drivers, and the bridge is less safe than it was before you improved anything. You optimised a module in isolation and degraded the system. The lights were hegemonica. You should have left them alone.

Some modules are working well. Some are working badly. Most are working fine. The ones working fine are hegemonica — not exciting, not perfect, just quietly doing their job. Your accounting software. Your commute. The way your team handles invoicing. The part of your diet that isn't causing problems.

The modules that are working badly are the ones that need your attention. They are the bottleneck — the constraint that limits the entire system's ability to achieve its purpose. Everything downstream of the bottleneck is waiting. Everything upstream is backing up. The system's output is capped not by its best module but by its worst.
The Limiting Factor

The bottleneck has another name: the limiting factor. It is the single constraint that, if removed, would allow the entire system to operate at a higher level. Not a slightly higher level — a categorically higher level, because everything that was waiting behind the bottleneck is suddenly free to move.

Improving a module that isn't the limiting factor produces a small, local improvement that doesn't change the system's output. The improvement is real but irrelevant — like widening a six-lane highway when the traffic jam is caused by a two-lane bridge at the end of it. You can widen the highway as much as you want. The bridge is the limiting factor. Until you fix the bridge, nothing changes.

This is why the shared-drive man is wasting his time. The shared drive is not the limiting factor. It never was. Somewhere in the system there is a two-lane bridge — a process, a person, a piece of infrastructure — that is constraining everything, and nobody is working on it because it's harder to fix than a folder structure.

Finding the limiting factor and removing it is the single highest-value action available in any system. It is not one improvement among many. It is the improvement that unlocks all the others. Every other improvement you could make is waiting behind it, trapped, unable to generate value until the constraint is gone.

I consider this to be of religious significance. Not metaphorically. If there is a practice worth devoting yourself to — a discipline that justifies the patience and focus and sacrifice required to master it — it is the practice of identifying the limiting factor and removing it. In your business, in your relationships, in your health, in your life. Find the thing that is holding the system back. Fix that. Everything else will follow.
The shared-drive mistake is spending your finite energy improving a module that was already fine while the limiting factor sits untouched, quietly capping the output of everything around it.
Why We Do It Anyway
Improving the wrong thing is not stupidity. It's psychology. There are very good reasons why people gravitate toward optimising modules that don't need it.

The working module is familiar. You understand it. You can see exactly how to make it better. The broken module is intimidating. It might involve confrontation, or admitting a mistake, or learning something new. The working module offers a guaranteed small win. The broken module offers an uncertain large win. Most people will choose the guaranteed small win every time, because human brains are wired to avoid uncertainty, even when the uncertain option is obviously more important.

There's also a status component. Reorganising the shared drive is visible. Everyone sees you doing it. Fixing the Thursday overtime problem might involve a quiet conversation with a difficult manager, and nobody sees quiet conversations. The optimiser gets credit for activity. The problem-solver gets credit for results, but only after the results materialise, which might take months.

This is why companies can have dozens of people working very hard on things that don't matter while one critical problem goes unaddressed. Everyone is busy. Everyone is improving something. The tragedy is that most of them are improving the wrong thing.
The Diagnostic
Two questions will save you more time than any productivity system ever invented.

First: what is the limiting factor? Not what annoys you most, not what's easiest to fix, not what you noticed this morning — what is the single constraint that, if removed, would unlock the most value? This is where your attention belongs.

Second: is the thing I'm about to improve actually the limiting factor, or is it just not perfect? If it's not the constraint — if it works, if people aren't complaining, if it isn't the thing capping the system's output — then it's hegemonica. Leave it alone. It has earned your respect by functioning quietly, and the kindest thing you can do for a working module is to ignore it and go fix the one that's holding everything back.
The Permission

There is a particular relief in declaring something good enough. Not as a surrender, but as a deliberate act of allocation. You are not saying "this can't be better." You are saying "this doesn't need to be better right now, and my energy is finite, and there is something over there that is actually failing."

Your desk is a bit messy but you know where everything is. Hegemonica. Your morning routine could be more efficient but it gets you to work on time. Hegemonica. Your company's website is slightly outdated but it converts customers at an acceptable rate. Hegemonica.

Meanwhile: your sleep is terrible. Your onboarding process loses 30% of new hires. Your relationship with your biggest client is deteriorating. These are not hegemonica. These are where your attention belongs.
The shared drive can wait. It was always fine. Go find the limiting factor. It's the only thing that matters.
